original_abstract: We report a highly enantioselective, radical-mediated hydroamination of enol esters with sulfonamides, enabled by the synergistic integration of an iridium photocatalyst, a Brønsted base, and a tetrapeptide thiol. This method facilitates the synthesis of 23 protected β-amino-alcohol derivatives with enantiomeric ratios as high as 97:3. The absolute stereochemistry is established via selective hydrogen atom transfer from the chiral thiol catalyst to a prochiral, carbon-centered radical intermediate. Comprehensive structure-selectivity analyses, involving systematic modifications to both the peptide scaffold and the olefin substrate, offered critical insights that guided the optimization of a high-performance catalyst. Combined experimental and computational mechanistic investigations suggest that enantioinduction and substrate recognition are driven by an intricate network of hydrogen bonding, π-π stacking, and London dispersion interactions. These results expand the scope of radical-based asymmetric catalysis and deepen our understanding of the noncovalent forces that govern complex chemical transformations.
humanized_abstract: We describe the iridium(III) photocatalytic, Brønsted base activated, tetrapeptide thiol catalyzed hydroamination of enol esters by radicals with sulfonamides to produce a variety of protected β-amino-alcohol derivatives in an enantioselectivity of up to 97% ee and in 23 examples in total. The absolute configuration of the products is controlled by a selective hydrogen atom transfer from the chiral thiol catalyst to a prochiral carbon-centred radical intermediate. A detailed structure–selectivity relationship within the peptide backbone and with respect to the olefin substrate provided the key information to design a high-activity catalyst system. The experimental findings, complemented by computational studies, reveal that the enantiodetermining steps of the catalytic cycle and the substrate binding events are governed by a complex interplay of hydrogen bonding, π-π stacking and London dispersion interactions. This work not only demonstrates the possibilities of radical-mediated asymmetric hydroamination but also sheds new light into the non-covalent interactions that dominate the outcome of highly complex catalytic reactions.

The absolute configuration of the products is controlled by a selective hydrogen atom transfer from the chiral thiol catalyst to a prochiral carbon-centred radical intermediate. A detailed structure–selectivity relationship within the peptide backbone and with respect to the olefin substrate provided the key information to design a high-activity catalyst system. The experimental findings, complemented by computational studies, reveal that the enantiodetermining steps of the catalytic cycle and the substrate binding events are governed by a complex interplay of hydrogen bonding, π-π stacking and London dispersion interactions. This work not only demonstrates the possibilities of radical-mediated asymmetric hydroamination but also sheds new light into the non-covalent interactions that dominate the outcome of highly complex catalytic reactions.
